content:
Welcome back folks. We are dealing with commodities, so it is very important you read the disclaimers here
and understand and remember that I am not a CTA and not a commodity trade adviser. Everything we
discuss here is only referring to a paper trade capacity only. Welcome back ICT mentorship June 2017
commodity trading lesson number two. We are going to be talking about relative strength analysis and we
can see professional accumulation and distribution.

- AGRICULTURAL
When we deal with commodities, there are two main camps or groups of markets that make up the
commodity market: the Agricultural and the Financial Market.
First in the agriculturals are the grains. I will tell you the ones that I like to follow. There are other grains
obviously like oats and such, but I am not going to concern myself with that. For the grain markets, we have
corn, wheat, and soybeans, and they trade on the Chicago Board of Trade. It is an exchange for the grain
markets. There are other grains that can be traded like canola and oats and something like that, but they
are very thin markets. These are the three that I historically as a commod Trader back in the early 90s,
these are the three I actually started to follow a lot.

- LIVESTOCK, FOODS, AND FIBERS
Out of the livestock sector of the Agricultural markets, we have feeder cattle, lean hogs, and live cattle.
Out of the foods area, we have Coco, orange juice (which is kind of thin but I include it when it is really a
freeze like in Florida; if there is an issue with the crop down in Florida, it is a really fun market to be trading
in, but otherwise orange juice is kind of thin but I include it because it is one of my favorites to watch for
that very reason), coffee, and then sugar.
And in the fibers, we have cotton. Now there is Lumber Market, but it is kind of thin as well.

- FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS
As the financial instruments, we have the debt instruments, which is the 30-year Treasury bond, 10-year
Treasury note, and 5-year Treasury note.
The currencies, the commdolls here, we see Aussie dollar, Canadian dollar, Japanese Yen, British pound,
Swiss Franc, euro dollar, and New Zealand dollar.
The metals we have gold, silver, and high-grade copper. Now there are two other metals, palladium and
platinum, that I did follow early on in the 90s, but I just fell out of interest in it, and these are the three
metals I like to follow. And the energies obviously we have crude oil.

section_title: BASKET OF COMMODITIES AND TRADER FOCUS
content:
When we look at the commodity market as a whole, this would be your basket of commodities to follow.
Now much like forex, there is a nice sample size to choose from which one you want to be trading and
which one you may want to be a specialist in. I liked the Treasury bond in the currency markets and
dabbled in gold and silver and copper when there was a real strong tendency for it to move. In the
agriculturals, I like soybeans, wheat, and feeder cattle and live cattle. And on a seasonal tendency, I like the
lean hogs. I traded cocoa, orange juice, coffee, sugar, and cotton.

So when we look at the commodity market and we look for relative strength analysis, relative strength
analysis is basically factoring your analysis around the most important market to be following right now
when you are bullish and the most important market to be following when you are bearish. And there are
really two simple ways to come to the conclusion of what that is.

As you know, the Dollar Index is like the king. Okay, so the greenback is the king. So what the dollar does
sets the tone for everything else, especially when it comes to commodities. So if we look for the Dollar
Index to be trading higher, that is going to put pressure on the commodity prices. If the Dollar Index is going
to be trading lower, that is going to allow and insight higher commodity prices. So when we look at this, I
want you to think in terms of that scenario.

section_title: INSTITUTIONALLY SPONSORED RALLIES AND SMT DIVERGENCE
content:
So when we look at institutionally sponsored rallies, what we are actually looking at is a market that has
failed to go lower at a time when the Dollar Index would be expected to go lower. So in other words, the
dollar is making some important high or maybe even a topping formation that may be a precursor to a
bullish commodity market. And the commodities that you would be looking to be long in are a commodity
that fails to make a lower low, which is basically an SMT Divergence.

So what we see here is in the graphic I diagram on the right hand side is underlying price strength. Now
this is what you want to classically see when you be buying commodities. What you are seeing is when the
market tends to trade higher energetically as a leader market, it establishes the underlying direction that is
opposite to the Dollar Index. Now these markets are extremely profitable and our aim is to focus on these
particular commodities to trade because they are going to be the strongest upside movers. Short term
highs are going to be seen broken and declines are going to be shallow in nature. Every time price makes
an upswing or makes an up closing candle, they are typically much larger than those that close lower or
any short-term swings that move lower in price.

The key is you want to be focusing on the commodities that fail to make a lower low. They are basically
diverging with the Dollar Index. So if we see that, we know that there is a professional accumulation, or in
this case institutional sponsorship for buying. If the institutions step in and because they are going to be
buying a whole lot more, because their institutional volume is much larger than a speculator would be like
you and I, that will facilitate the market rallying higher and showing unwillingness to go lower because it is a
large degree of buying coming in. Well, there the supply and demand factors are coming into force with the
commodity. They are not going to permit price to go lower. So if it will fail to make a lower low when the
Dollar Index makes that higher high, you have to understand what the Dollar Index should be doing as a
whole though.

section_title: DOLLAR INDEX CONTEXT FOR COMMODITY MOVES
content:
Because it may not be so clear just by looking at higher highs and lower lows in commodities. When one
diverges, it does not necessarily mean that there is a trade. You have to have the analysis on your Dollar
Index because that is going to set the tone for a prolonged move. If the dollar is going to weaken, that is
going to provide at least the intermediate term basis for the commodity market as a whole to rally, but not
every commodity is going to rally. This is what fits out these strong moves and which is why we say it is
relative strength analysis. Relatively speaking, a commodity that fails to make a lower low like this, it is
stronger because it has failed to make a lower low. So when the dollar drops or shows weakness, this
particular commodity, whatever whichever one it would be that creates this condition or scenario, that is the
one we would be looking for bullish ideas to unfold in.

Now as we just discussed in the beginning of this teaching, I have broken down the agricultural sector and
the financial sector of the commodity markets. So we have a basket of commodities that we can follow in
two divisions: one, the agricultural markets (things that are grown or living), and then we have the
synthetics (the things that are manmade or created or manufactured or drawn from the earth that do not
breathe, like oil and energies). So by having these two divisions in the commodity markets, we have a nice
basket of choice to make. If there is a bias to the market as a whole for the commodity market to go higher,
that means that the Dollar Index should be going lower or expected to go lower. Not every commodity is
going to go higher. The ones that fail to make that lower low, that is the one we are really focusing on. Okay,
so that is really the heart of this teaching.

But as we saw, there are different groups of commodities like the livestock and then there are the grains.
There are currencies. When we see those little groups of types of markets, like again all the meats like
feeder cattle, live cattle, and lean hogs, those three make up the livestock group. One of those meats may
fail to make a lower low when the dollar is starting to go lower. And when it does that, it is showing
leadership. For instance, it could be the live cattle market, and feeder cattle may not have that higher low
form. It may make a lower low like it is being described here graphically on this chart, and maybe lean hogs
makes a lower low as well, but live cattle makes that higher low we just shown in the previous slide. And
institutional sponsorship behind that particular commodity would send live cattle much higher and a lot
more magnitude and speed because you can see that they are buying that one up.

The other meats or other commodities and other groups that may not show those evidences of a higher low
when the dollar is weakening, that would be seen as this and you would be seeing a sympathetic rally.
Okay, and what we are seeing is typically a commodity that makes a lower low but starts to trade higher
sympathetically. So we have sympathetic price strength. Now this is going to be a market that tends to
trade higher in sympathy to the leader market that establishes the underlying direction. While these
markets are potentially profitable, again our aim is to focus on the leadership markets for the strongest
rallies. Now short-term highs are seen broken and declines are shallow in nature and the upswings and up
closing candles much like we saw with the institutionally sponsored rally, they are typically going to be
larger than those that close lower or move lower.

So if you are trading a commodity that has the characteristics that is being described here, you are not
actually buying the relative strength leader on the buy side. You are actually buying a sympathetic rally or a
the six sister of the group. Okay? And not that you cannot make money doing that, because you can see
that many times those moves will be moving in sympathy and moving in tandem, but at a lesser degree in
terms of speed, magnitude, and it will be more lethargic. It may get to objectives that you trade and reach
for, but not as strong and as quick and as efficiently that you would be seeing it with the leadership.

Obviously the opposite is seen here. Institutionally sponsored decline, it is a market that tends to trade
lower energetically as a leader market and establishes the underlying direction opposite to the Dollar Index.
These markets are extremely profitable and our aim is to focus on these markets as they will be the
strongest downside movers. Short-term lows are going to be seen broken and rallies are going to be
shallow in nature. Down swings in price and or the down closing candles are typically much larger than
those that show with a closing higher or upswing in price.

The key on this again much like we saw with the institutional sponsored rally is the focal point of the failure
swing to make a higher high. When that Dollar Index is bullish and it is about to rally, that is going to put
underlying pressure on commodities as a whole. Not all the commodities are going to drop. The ones that
fail to make a higher high, they are going to be leadership on the downside. So they are going to be
showing underlying price weakness.

Much like we saw with the sympathetic rally, we have a sympathetic decline. This is a market that tends to
trade lower in sympathy to the leader market that establishes the underlying downside direction. While
these markets are potentially profitable, again our aim is to focus on the leadership market for the weakest
declines. Just like we saw with the sympathetic rally, we can see short-term lows are being broken and
rallies are shallow in nature. The downside swings and down closed candles are typically much larger than
those that close higher or the moves higher. Just everything reversed when we saw for the slide for the
sympathetic rally.

section_title: BEARISH LEADER VS. SYMPATHETIC DECLINE COMPARISON
content:
Basically what we are seeing is price weakness in a sympathetic nature to the leadership. Again I will use
this analogy, say the soybean market was the leadership issue on the downside, it failed to make a higher
high while the grain complex should be moving lower as a whole. Say wheat makes a higher high and corn
makes a higher high, but soybean makes that lower high. Well, soybeans is going to be the outperformer
on the downside. It does not mean that wheat and corn won't decline, but it will be doing so in a
sympathetic fashion like we are describing here. Again, we would be focusing on the weakest of the
commodities to sell short, and the way you determine is by looking at their highs.

section_title: DOLLAR INDEX FROM MID-2016 TO JUNE 2017
content:
All right, so last, let us go through the commodities as a basket and we will start figuring out what we see
generally when the Dollar Index is giving us indications that it wants to move higher or lower. First area of
business is the middle of 2016's summer. We saw the dollar rallying up into January 2017 making a high.
And then we saw post-January 2017, we have been seeing continued weakness on the dollar. So from the
middle of the summer of 2016 to the beginning of this year in 2017, we would expect to see commodities
going lower while the dollar has been moving higher. And then obviously commodities from January to the
present time of this recording June 2017, we should be expecting commodities to rally or look for
leadership issues to fail to make lower lows and then look for opportunities to be a buyer as a paper Trader
in those particular commodities. And that way you will learn how these commodity markets work.

- CORN
First one we are looking at here is corn. We are going to go through the agriculturals first, and corn is our
first grain. And you can see that from the middle of 2016, we saw the decline in corn as we would expect
with a higher dollar. And down below the August and September lows, we can see corn kept making a
lower low, which was underlying weakness while the Dollar Index was making higher highs. The corn
market showed a willingness to go higher off of a higher low, so we had a failure swing going into the latter
portions of December 2016, but we had extreme weakness shown in the move down. But December into
January, we saw the higher low form and the market went relatively sideways. Small little modest move
from 360 up to 390, which is only a 30-cent move. In this case, it would be about $1,500 per contract. So
that is actually a very weak market. The grain market for this specific commodity, corn, was weak. We did
not see any kind of leadership buying in here at all.

- WHEAT
The wheat market, much like corn, we saw this slide down from the summer months making lower lows
and then making a lower low even in December going into January. So we had continued weakness on
wheat. This grain stayed in a consolidation as well, failed to make any kind of real significant rally higher,
and its underlying weakness was seen in price action.

- SOYBEANS (LEADERSHIP)
Soybeans, we saw the summer decline going into August, but then August and September and October we
started seeing the soybean market fail to make lower lows while we saw the dollar making higher highs. So
there was already indications that the soybean market was under accumulation because it was failing to go
lower. In the second week of November going into January 2017, we saw soybean market trade down with
a higher low and actually talked about this during the mentorship. If you go back in the January kind of our
time together, you actually see me talk about that and mention those equal highs that were around that
1080 level on this chart and price rallied up and we saw relative strength in the bean complex. So soybeans
across the grain group was the leadership issue, so soybeans were strong. So if you were looking to be a
buyer in January when the Dollar Index was making significant reasons to look for a lower move in the
dollar that is going to allow commodities to rally, soybeans in this case rallies about $4,500 per contract in
terms of a move.

- FEEDER CATTLE (LEADERSHIP)
The next one we are going into the livestock group. Now just like we saw in the previous commodities, we
saw the decline from the summer months going down into the fall end of the year. But then we started
seeing some accumulation seen in the middle of October going into the first week of December. So while
the Dollar Index was making higher highs, the feeder cattle market was failing to go lower. So it was already
showing signs of accumulation. If it is not going lower, there is only one reason for that, it means that they
are under accumulation and they are buying it. Who? Institutions are large traders, big players, so they are
buying up feeder cattle. And notice that we are seeing short-term highs being broken and more short-term
highs being broken. And then at a later time in March, when the dollar creates another intermediate term
high and starts to sell off, we have that February March time period where the feeder cattle market trades
right back down into a bullish order block. So we have the accumulation pattern seen here, but early signs
were already shown that they were accumulating the feeder cattle market. Feeder cattle actually developed
a premium, which we will start teaching later on in this week, but the price of feeder cattle actually went in
this case parabolic, and it was very strong as a result of that because we saw price show signs that it is
wanting to not go lower. It was breaking short-term highs, it was under accumulation. And then at a later
time when Dollar Index was ready to sell off again in March of 2017, that provided another buying
opportunity for feeder cattle. Notice also the last half of February going into March, the Dollar Index was
making higher highs around that 102 level, but that last portion of February going into March on feeder
cattle was making a higher low. Again that same accumulation pattern it is being seen there, but it is also
occurring in a previous bullish order block. So we are blending PD arrays discount with the context that we
should be seeing higher commodity prices. This one fit the bill because it had everything in favor for it.
What does it translate into? This move here from the buy all the way to the high is over $19,000 per
contract of a move. Not too shabby.

- LEAN HOGS (LEADERSHIP)
All right, the next one in the livestock complex is lean hogs. We saw the decline from the summer months
going down into September and October, and then October, November, and December, we started seeing
accumulation patterns again. Market was failing to go lower while the Dollar Index was making higher
highs. We seen clear accumulation patterns in the lean hogs market. Later on in 2017, we had another
opportunity for the Dollar Index to sell off, traded up into a premium array, filled in a liquidity void. Price
trades lower on the Dollar Index, which is going to allow bullishness for commodities. Again, we can see in
the lean hogs market, the market trades back down into a bullish order block and we have a subsequent
parabolic rally from the 68 to 815 cents for lean hogs, and that means about $5,600 per contract.

- LIVE CATTLE (LEADERSHIP)
Last one in the livestock group is live cattle. Price trades down from the summer months into October
making a low, and then from October going into November and December, we see accumulation occurring
again in here. While the dollar is rallying, the live cattle market is failing to go lower, so they are under
massive accumulation. Same scenario we said earlier with the March high in the Dollar Index creating a
selloff. There is a buying opportunity in the live cattle market, trades down into a bullish order block, rallies
away. And again, much like we saw with the feeder cattle market, live cattle futures were very, very strong.
It translates to a move of $12,000 per contract.

- COCO (WEAKNESS)
All right, now we are going into the foods and the first on our list is Coco. Coco is clearly under a lot of
distribution. They had a bumper crop. There was no real shortage of cocoa, and there was no impulse for
the supply and demand factor to be swung to a shortage, so there was lots of supply of Coco, which
provided this consistent decline on the price of the Coco Futures. So we have no real indication that this is
under any accumulation whatsoever, and our analysis comes away with Coco is very weak. It is not under
accumulation, so this would never meet the criteria as a buy while the dollar is dropping.

- COFFEE (WEAKNESS)
Coffee, same scenario. We saw weakness, market making lower lows in December going into January as
well. We had a nice little pop up, which is a nice little retracement, but it overall kept in sync with the
weakness that was seen to coffee here, while weak as well.

- SUGAR (MIXED, ULTIMATELY WEAK)
And sugar is our next one. Again, we see in November going into the middle of December, we see sugar
making lower lows. We have a small little divergence at the lows that saw a rally up, but when we got back
up into February March, that found its weakness after coming up into a premium array or bullish order block
around that 2050 level, and this could not find any more bullishness. So in this case, we can see kind of like
a mixed signal. So you could have looked at it as weakness and then the last little portion of December
going into January that higher low while the Dollar Index actually made a higher high, that is a divergence
that would be a failure swing, so that is why you see that move higher up in sugar, but then it ultimately falls
over and cannot find any more continuation. So I want to provide you opportunities where in realistic terms
you are going to be able to see these things, but then you are also going to see things that start off like a
good move and then fail, and that is what real environments are going to be like for you. So in this case
here, sugar was weak.

- COTTON (LEADERSHIP)
Okay, we are in the fibers group and we only have cotton in our basket of commodities to be concerned
about. The slide from August down in September after initial rally trades back down into a bullish order
block at September. Dollar is rallying, Cotton is not going lower, and you start seeing successive higher
highs, higher lows. Every short-term high is finding its way broken with no problem at all and bullish order
blocks are supporting price. So we can see clearly that this commodity is under accumulation. So in this
case here, cotton was a very strong commodity at the time when the dollar was weak. As a result, the
move that transpires is over $8,000 per contract.

- DEBT INSTRUMENTS (WEAKNESS)
Okay, now we are in financials. We are looking at the 30-year Treasury bond, and I am actually going to talk
a lot more about bonds in the bond trading week. So you are going to see something in this chart that if you
are a student of mine, obviously you are going to know right away what it looks like and what we should be
talking about, but we are going to keep in general terms just for the commodity portion of this month. So we
see the market making lower lows here, underlying weakness. And the bond market has been weak. We
have only really traded in a consolidation, haven't really made any significant moves higher, which has
been the catalyst I believe that why the markets are so fickle right now. We have had so many issues with
finding sustained moves in the Foreign Exchange Market, and it is going to be translated by looking at the
chart here. You can see clearly that the market for the bonds have been range bound and they haven't
really moved much at all. And then we have the 10-year notes, same thing. We see underlying weakness,
10-years is weak as well, and we only saw a consolidation transpire since then. And five-year notes, we
actually saw weakness and then further weakness in March, and we have consolidated since then. So
nothing has really happened in debt instruments. And again, interest rates are what makes the markets go
around, so if we are a currency trader, it is going to put a damper if you will on how we see sustained
moves in the currency markets.

- AUSTRALIAN DOLLAR (LEADERSHIP)
Speaking of currencies, okay, the first on our list is the Australian dollar. Looking closely, look at the highs
on the Dollar Index going into January. We made that higher high in the Dollar Index. We made that failure
swing to go lower on the Australian dollar. If you go back in the January month, you will actually see me talk
about that very particular thing. There was weakness seen in the Dollar Index at those highs, and we saw
accumulation come in on the Australian dollar at those lows. Incidentally, Australian dollar was strong with a
differential, so the interest rate differential was a bonus for this one, so that is why we saw this nice
extrapolator move to the upside.

- CANADIAN DOLLAR (WEAKNESS)
The next one on our list is Canadian dollar, and this one here had basically an equal low and it started off
with basically a modest bullishness to it, but finally gave up the ghost in February and rolled right back over
and followed suit with the Dollar Index, which again is kind of weird because the Dollar Index and Canadian
dollar moving together makes it difficult.

- JAPANESE YEN (LEADERSHIP)
All right, so we have the Japanese Yen next. Again, look very closely at the Dollar Index. We made the
higher high going into January, but we have the higher low in the Japanese Yen. That is the accumulation
that is seen by way of institutional sponsored rally. So we can see the market was making short-term highs,
breaking those short-term highs, and order blocks that were bullish were being supporting in price. So Yen
in this case was very strong, so this was our leadership issue as well for the currency market, so it is
Australian dollar and Yen so far.

- BRITISH POUND (WEAKNESS)
Okay, we have the British pound here. This one has been lethargic. We have a slightly lower low going into
January from October. No real accumulation in this pair at all until we got around the March time period
when March creates the high in the Dollar Index. Then we saw the opportunity for the cable to start rallying
from that 122 level, but overall the pound market has been weak.

- SWISS FRANC (LEADERSHIP)
Okay, the next one here is the Swiss Franc, and look closely again, you will see that failure swing. So we
have institutional sponsorship rally, higher low when the Dollar Index makes a higher high. Weakness in the
Dollar Index should see the foreign currencies rally. Their commdolls, they should go higher. In this case
here, we see the Swiss Franc do that very thing and go higher. And at the March time period when we saw
the Dollar Index create a high, we have another intermediate term low on the Swiss franc and it rallies
again. And then in May last month, where we saw the Dollar Index trade up and close its fair value gap as a
premium array, the Swiss Franc creates a turtle suit long and closes in its fair value gap seen in the month
of March and then rallies higher. So we have a very strong Swiss Franc as a result. So we have the
Australian dollar, the Japanese Yen, and Swiss Franc as our leadership issues.

- EURO DOLLAR (LEADERSHIP)
And then we have the Euro Dollar. We have a higher low also on this currency. So this one here, we look
and see we have short-term highs being broken and all premium arrays being broken on the upside and
then supporting price at the discount arrays. So every bullish order block we see price being more
accumulation and more rallying, so Euro was very strong here as well.

- NEW ZEALAND DOLLAR (LEADERSHIP)
And last in our group of currencies for the commdolls, we see one more instance of a currency that had a
differential. Interest rate differential was a plus for this one as well. So in other words, if you buy the New
Zealand dollar in Alti, it paid you an interest rate for holding on to it for carry trade purposes, and those two
currencies were actually the leader of all the interest rate differentials at the time for this beginning of this
year. And with this higher low, which is institutional sponsored rally at the time when the institutions were
looking to sell short on the Dollar Index in January, made a higher high in dollar, did not see that lower low
we would expect in New Zealand with a differential as well. So here was kiwi as an upside leader as well.

- GOLD (LEADERSHIP)
Okay, moving over to the metals. Okay, we have another similar situation here where the gold market was
failing and we talked about this in January too. Gold was failing to make a lower low, so we have
institutional sponsored rally when the Dollar Index was making higher highs. And as a subsequent to that
occurrence, we see all the bullish order blocks providing support to price and premium arrays being broken
as price moves higher. Gold in this instance was a strong commodity to be a buyer.

- SILVER (MODESTLY STRONG)
Silver started the same way gold did, higher low, institutional sponsorship rally and price was reaching
through premium arrays, bullish order blocks were supporting price until we got up around that 1850 level
and it fell out of bed. It came all the way back down but it traded a bullish order block at that 16 level, which
I talked about at time of the decline. If we got down to that level, it would be an interesting buying
opportunity. And we have since then rallied 168 points, so almost $7,000 plus dollars per contract if you
were looking to trade that 16 level. But it was modestly strong here. And as a note, I actually was very, very
bullish on Silver much more than gold, but for whatever reason it failed at the 1850 level and the storyline
changed from that point on.

- HIGH-GRADE COPPER (STRONG)
Okay, the last metal we are going to look at is high-grade copper. And you can see the accumulation that
was really underway in September and October, failing to make a lower low and finally just exploded on the
upside. And copper here was a very strong metal as well.

- CRUDE OIL (STRONG)
And finally for the energy complex in our commodity basket, we have crude oil. And crude oil was failing to
make lower lows as the Dollar Index was rallying. And this particular energy was under accumulation
around the $43 a barrel mark and then finally rallied up. And you can see crude oil was a strong commodity
as well.

section_title: SUMMARY OF RELATIVE STRENGTH ANALYSIS FOR COMMODITIES
content:
So what we have done is we have gone through all the commodities and we use relative strength analysis
to ferret out the leadership issues when the market should be seeing commodities trade higher. Notice that
all of them did not trade higher, but we were able to go in looking at price action, no indicators, no bells and
whistles, just simply understanding what the pattern looks like when large institutions step in and buy. What
does that pattern look like? It is that failure swing. It is the fact that short-term highs are being broken and
premium arrays are not respecting anything in terms of offering resistance. It is being broken through, and
then all of the discount arrays are supporting price. Everything that we have shown here is reversed when
the Dollar Index is bullish and we would be looking for bearish ideas in commodities, and we would go
through the commodity markets and look at everything that we did here just in opposite terms.

But for agriculturals, what we have done is we ferreted out the leadership from the grains: it was the
soybean market. Out of the livestock, all three of them did well, but feeder cattle was by far the absolute
leader. Feeder cattle actually developed a premium, which we will teach later on in this week about, and we
actually use it as an example as well. But the feeder cattle market was the upside leader. On the foods,
notice there was an absence of really anything that lent well for a buying opportunity. Out of all the foods,
they were all relatively weak. The fibers where again we have Lumber is in that group, but we do not look at
Lumber because it is kind of too thin in my opinion. Cotton was an outperformer on the upside as well
because it was living underneath professional accumulation. So we ferreted out one, two, three, four, five
markets out of all the agricultural markets. Our trading patterns and price action analysis concept helps us
find where the leadership issues are going to be as a buying opportunity.
Financials, we did not see anything in the debt instruments, and I have been saying this all year long, which
is the reason why the markets have been pretty much next to untradeable. The currencies, we see that
Australian dollar, the Yen, the Frank, the Euro, and the Kiwi were all upside leadership. In the metals, all
three of them did very, very well here. Silver was the weakest sister of them all, admittedly, but gold was the
outperformer in my opinion. We did have a lot of strength on copper, but you only had one real opportunity
to get in that one, so that is why I am saying gold was the outperformer because gold gave you a couple
different instances to get in. And lastly, in our basket of commodities is crude oil, and we saw that one also
provide a nice opportunity to be a buyer. Nothing really to compare it to. It used to be heating oil and
unleaded gas and natural gas I would look at when I was actually looking at commodities as a major asset
class and that was the only thing I was trading at the time. But if you are going to have a basket of
commodities to watch, I think this is a very good one. It is well balanced, and it gives you a nice universe to
work within. It gives you an ability to work within multiple groups to look for leadership issues.
CONCLUSION:

section_title: THE POWER OF RELATIVE STRENGTH ANALYSIS
content:
And as we indicated and shown here in this teaching, not all commodities are equal. They are not going to
perform the same. You have to have tools and concepts to go through and as a process eliminate the ones
that are not performing as you would expect in price action and focus on the ones that are giving you that
fingerprint hallmark signature that there is huge accumulation underway and or distribution. So by using
this concept, it will help ferret out all the better moving opportunities when the markets are predisposed to
go in one direction or another. Until next lesson, I wish you good luck and good trading.
